learning: Multi-phase tasks fail ~60% of the time. Todo completion rate is only 40%. Expect to finish 4 of 10 steps.
Memory's greatest value is storing decisions and preferences, not code patterns. Corrections and non-obvious constraints are most valuable.
Moving project directories fragments Claude Code context. Internal-Tracker split across 3 paths created 3 separate memory silos.
Testing is consistently the most deferred step. It should be Phase 1, not the last step.
Plans are the most valuable persistent artifact. 300+ line architectural blueprints survive across sessions and serve as roadmaps.
Pre-approving common commands in permissions.allow saves hundreds of confirmation clicks per week.
97% of todo files are empty arrays. The system initializes for every agent/subagent but is rarely used meaningfully.
Security warnings are domain-blind. Serialization in ML and child_process in agent runtimes are architectural necessities, not vulnerabilities.
File-history uses full snapshots not diffs. 173 session dirs with multiple versions can consume gigabytes over time.
Even with DISABLE_TELEMETRY=1, events are still generated and written to disk. Files up to 23MB each. Safe to delete.
140K messages in 51 days. Cache read ratio 4.6B read vs 257M created shows excellent prompt caching efficiency.
Peak productivity at midnight IST (19:00 UTC). 36 sessions at that hour versus minimal usage 2-8 AM.
Session-env is completely unused dead weight. 609 empty directories that can be safely deleted.
CCS delegation skill enables cost arbitrage by routing Haiku calls to GPT-5.4 and keeping Opus for heavy thinking.
Duplicate todo lists appear across sessions. Same documentation tasks attempted 3+ times means work was abandoned and restarted.
Always verify actual SDK exports and types against installed code before proposing approaches.
User prefers Django 6 + Templates + Tailwind + HTMX + Alpine.js as target stack for web projects.
User rejected Vue.js in favor of Vanilla JS for Tour2Tech PMO. Framework preference varies by project.
